2605.03212.title: ADAPTS: Agentic Decomposition for Automated Protocol-agnostic Tracking of Symptoms
2605.03212.paper_summary: Modeling latent clinical constructs from unconstrained clinical interactions is a unique challenge in affective computing. We present ADAPTS (Agentic Decomposition for Automated Protocol-agnostic Tracking of Symptoms), a framework for automated rating of depression and anxiety severity using a mixture-of-agents LLM architecture. This approach decomposes long-form clinical interviews into symptom-specific reasoning tasks, producing auditable justifications while preserving temporal and speaker alignment. Generalization was evaluated across two independent datasets ($N=204$) with distinct interview structures. On high-discrepancy interviews, automated ratings approximated expert benchmarks ($\text{absolute error}=22$) more closely than original human ratings ($\text{absolute error}=26$). Implementing an ``extended'' protocol that incorporates qualitative clinical conventions significantly stabilized ratings, with absolute agreement reaching $\text{ICC(2,1)} = 0.877$. These findings suggest that the ADAPTS framework enables promising evaluations of psychiatric severity. While the current implementation is purely text-based, the underlying architecture is readily extensible to multimodal inputs, including acoustic and visual features. By approximating expert-level precision in a protocol-agnostic manner, this framework provides a foundation for objective and scalable psychiatric assessment, especially in resource-limited settings.
2605.03212.paper_summary_zh: 從不受限制的臨床互動中建模潛在的臨床構念是情感計算中的一個獨特挑戰。我們提出了 ADAPTS（自動化無協議症狀追蹤的代理分解），這是一個使用混合代理 LLM 架構自動評估抑鬱和焦慮嚴重程度的框架。這種方法將長篇臨床訪談分解為特定症狀的推理任務，生成可審計的理由，同時保持時間和說話者的對齊。在兩個獨立的數據集（$N=204$）中評估了泛化，這些數據集具有不同的訪談結構。在高差異的訪談中，自動評分比原始人類評分更接近專家基準（$\text{absolute error}=22$ 對比 $\text{absolute error}=26$）。實施一個“擴展”協議，納入定性臨床慣例，顯著穩定了評分，絕對一致性達到 $\text{ICC(2,1)} = 0.877$。這些發現表明，ADAPTS 框架能夠進行有前景的精神病學嚴重程度評估。儘管當前的實施完全基於文本，但其底層架構可以輕鬆擴展到多模態輸入，包括聲學和視覺特徵。通過以無協議的方式接近專家級精度，這一框架為客觀和可擴展的精神病學評估提供了基礎，特別是在資源有限的環境中。
2605.03212.author: Alexandria K. Vail et.al.
2605.03212.authors: Alexandria K. Vail, Marcelo Cicconet, Katie Aafjes-van Doorn, Ryan Maroney, Marc Aafjes
